title: Hybrid Nanophotonic Scintillators for Enhanced X-ray Absorption, Emission, and Time Resolution
authors: Seou Choi, Sachin Vaidya, Avner Shultzman, Charles Roques-Carmes, Ido Kaminer, Marin Soljačić
abstract: Scintillators convert ionizing radiation into visible photons, enabling applications from cosmic ray detection to medical imaging. Two independent strategies for improving scintillator performance via nanoscale patterning have recently been demonstrated: engineering material properties to enhance absorption of ionizing radiation and integrating nanophotonic structures to enhance the spontaneous emission rate ("nanophotonic scintillators"). Here, we propose a nanophotonic scintillator that simultaneously enhances both the initial energy conversion and the spontaneous emission rate, by periodically stacking a fast-emitting scintillator and a visible-light-transparent material with strong X-ray attenuation ("stopping layer") to form a one-dimensional (1D) photonic crystal (PhC) scintillator. Photoelectric absorption in the stopping layer increases the number of photoelectrons that deposit energy in neighboring scintillator layers and contribute to scintillation. At the same time, the spontaneous emission rate is enhanced by the nanophotonic structuring itself. We design a 1D PhC comprising an organic scintillator and indium tin oxide (ITO) as the stopping layer and numerically simulate the enhancement in scintillation yield and decay rate. The total detected light output is enhanced by up to a factor of 700 compared to a bulk organic scintillator of equal thickness. We further investigate a 1D PhC structure integrating inorganic and organic scintillators for time-of-flight positron emission tomography (TOF-PET): replacing the non-scintillating stopping layer with an inorganic scintillator further increases the light yield, and the coincidence time resolution (CTR) is enhanced up to 3.5 times compared to a bulk inorganic scintillator of equal thickness. Our work presents a unified approach to improve key scintillation parameters within a single nanophotonic structure.
summary: The researchers developed a hybrid nanophotonic scintillator that uses a 1D photonic crystal structure to simultaneously boost X-ray absorption and emission speed. By interleaving high-Z stopping layers with organic scintillators, they achieved massive increases in light yield and timing resolution. This approach could significantly advance the performance of medical imaging technologies like PET scans.
main_problem: The challenge of simultaneously achieving high X-ray absorption (high light yield) and fast decay times (short emission) in scintillators, which are traditionally subject to a performance trade-off.
main_result: A 1D photonic crystal structure can enhance total detected light output by up to 700-fold and improve Coincidence Time Resolution (CTR) by up to 3.5 times compared to bulk materials.
method: The study uses Geant4 Monte Carlo simulations for X-ray attenuation and electron transport, combined with numerical modeling of the Purcell effect and photon outcoupling efficiency.
model_or_system: A 1D photonic crystal (PhC) scintillator consisting of periodically stacked layers of a fast-emitting organic scintillator and a high-Z, visible-light-transparent stopping layer (such as ITO or inorganic scintillators).
key_observables: Light output/yield, scintillation decay time, Coincidence Time Resolution (CTR), and X-ray attenuation length.
important_parameters: Subwavelength layer thickness (nanoscale), electron diffusion length, refractive index contrast, and effective atomic number (Zeff).
main_assumptions: The model assumes photoelectrons can escape nanoscale stopping layers into adjacent scintillators and that visible light absorption in the stopping layer is negligible.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the schematic of the photoelectric-enhanced mechanism; Fig 2 compares X-ray attenuation and electron diffusion, showing enhancement metrics as a function of layer thickness; Fig 3 compares light output and CTR between the hybrid PhC and bulk LSO.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of scintillator trade-offs, proposes a 1D PhC design, details the physical mechanisms (photoelectric effect and Purcell effect), presents numerical simulation results for both organic/ITO and inorganic/organic systems, and discusses fabrication and scaling.
why_interesting: The paper utilizes fundamental quantum optical phenomena, specifically the Purcell effect, to manipulate spontaneous emission rates and photon extraction in a structured dielectric environment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Photoelectric-enhanced nanophotonic scintillators. When an X-ray hits the stopping layer (e.g., indium tin oxide (ITO)), it gen- erates electrons through the photoelectric effect. High-energy photo- electrons that can escape the stopping layer enter the scintillator and generate visible photons. Scintillators with weak X-ray absorption such as organic scintillators can significantly enhance the scintilla- tion light output with the aid of stopping layers. The thickness of the stopping layer and the scintillator layer are nanoscale, enabling faster scintillation via the Purcell enhancement.

FIG. 2. Scintillation performance enhancement in 1D photonic crystal (PhC) scintillators. (a) Linear attenuation length of indium tin oxide (ITO) and organic scintillator as a function of X-ray energy. (b) Diffusion length of electrons as a function of energy. (c) Number of scintillation events inside the 1D scintillator structure numerically calculated as a function of the thickness of unit layer under different incident X-ray energies. The total thickness is 10 µm, and the thickness of each ITO and organic scintillator are set to be equal dscint = dITO = 10/2Num, where 2N is the number of layers (N = 1, 2, 3, ...). Dashed lines in the right panel show the 1% of the X-ray attenuation...

FIG. 3. Physical mechanism of hybrid PhC scintillators. (a) Concept of hybrid PhC scintillators. Integrating organic and inorganic scintillators allows faster and brighter scintillation. (b) Attenuation coefficient of BGO and organic scintillator as a function of incident ionizing radiation energy. (c) Number of detected photons as a function of arrival time for different scintillators. Hybrid I has dinorganic = 150 nm, dorganic = 50 nm, and hybrid II has dinorganic = 70 nm, dorganic = 130 nm. Except for LSO having ∼9 µm to give equal X-ray attenuation length as hybrid II, all structures are 20 µm thick. (d) Enhancement of CTR as a function of dorganic,inorganic.
